subject: CS-HCI
description: Define the initial scope and high-level plan for the adaptive UI research project. This involves formulating the primary research questions about adapting UI layouts to user context and evaluating the impact on usability and user satisfaction.
reason: This is the inaugural event that bootstraps the project. It takes the abstract, pre-existing idea of the project's purpose as an initial 'research_goals' requirement and formalizes it into a concrete artifact, which is then used to create the first 'research_plan'.

subject: CS-HCI
description: Design the initial experimental methodology. This includes selecting the specific adaptive algorithm, defining the user context variables (e.g., task complexity, device type), and choosing the metrics for evaluating UI effectiveness (e.g., task completion time, subjective satisfaction).
reason: Following the initial proposal (e_01), the next logical step is to translate the abstract research goals and plan into a concrete experimental design. This 'method_scheme' is essential before any pilot or main experiments can be conducted.

subject: CS-HCI
description: Conduct a small-scale pilot study to validate the experimental setup. A few participants are recruited to test the adaptive UI prototype, allowing the team to identify technical issues, assess task difficulty, and get preliminary feedback on the user experience.
reason: With a concrete 'method_scheme' from e_02, the logical next step is to run a pilot study. This is a crucial risk-mitigation step to test the feasibility and robustness of the experimental design before committing the resources required for the main experiments. The pilot will produce the first set of 'experiment_results'.

subject: CS-HCI
description: Analyze the data collected from the pilot study. The analysis reveals that while the adaptive mechanism works, the chosen tasks were too simple, leading to a ceiling effect in performance metrics and making it hard to detect significant differences.
reason: After conducting a pilot study (e_03) and obtaining the first 'experiment_results', the immediate next step is to perform an 'analysis'. This is crucial for interpreting the pilot data to validate the 'method_scheme' and determine if it's robust enough for the main study. The findings from this analysis will guide the decision on whether to proceed or to revise the experimental design.

subject: CS-HCI
description: Revise the experimental design in response to the pilot study's findings. The team redesigns the user tasks to be more complex and varied, aiming to eliminate the ceiling effect observed in the performance metrics. Minor adjustments are also made to the data logging mechanism to capture more nuanced user interactions.
reason: The analysis of the pilot study (e_04) identified a critical flaw in the experimental setup (tasks were too simple). Therefore, the logical next step is to return to the 'scheme_design' phase to revise the 'method_scheme'. This iterative process of refining the methodology based on pilot data is a crucial part of ensuring the main experiments will yield valid and meaningful results.

subject: CS-HCI
description: While preparing for the main experiment, the team begins to draft the methodology section of the research paper. This captures the rationale behind the revised experimental design, including the choice of adaptive algorithm, task design, and evaluation metrics.
reason: Following the revision of the experimental setup (e_05), the 'method_scheme' is now sufficiently mature. It is an opportune moment to begin the 'writing' process by drafting the methods section. This documents the design decisions before the main experiment begins and can be done in parallel with participant recruitment.

subject: CS-HCI
description: With the revised methodology in place, the team launches the main experiment. A larger and more diverse group of participants is recruited to interact with the adaptive UI under various conditions, generating a comprehensive dataset on performance and user satisfaction.
reason: After revising the experimental design (e_05) based on the pilot study's findings (e_04), the next logical step is to conduct the 'main_experiments'. This phase is critical for collecting the primary data needed to address the core 'research_goals' using the validated 'method_scheme'.

subject: CS-HCI
description: The team performs a comprehensive analysis of the data collected from the main experiment. Statistical tests are run to evaluate the hypotheses regarding the effectiveness of the adaptive UI compared to a static baseline, focusing on performance metrics and user satisfaction scores.
reason: After successfully conducting the main experiment (e_07) and collecting a large dataset, the most critical next step is to perform a thorough 'analysis'. This will process the new 'experiment_results' to determine if the research hypotheses (from 'research_goals') are supported, providing the core findings of the study.

subject: CS-HCI
description: Draft the results section of the research paper. This involves summarizing the key findings from the main experiment's analysis, creating visualizations to illustrate the data, and interpreting the statistical outcomes in the context of the research questions.
reason: After performing the comprehensive analysis of the main experiment (e_08), the most logical next step is to document these findings. This 'writing' event translates the processed 'experiment_results' into the results section of the paper, building upon the previously drafted methods section and moving the project closer to dissemination.

subject: CS-HCI
description: While drafting the results, the team notes a surprising trade-off: the adaptive UI improved performance but decreased user satisfaction. To investigate this, a follow-up qualitative analysis is performed on open-ended user comments collected during the main experiment to identify themes explaining the dissatisfaction.
reason: The analysis in e_08 and subsequent writing in e_09 revealed a complex, mixed outcome. Before writing the paper's discussion, a deeper qualitative 'analysis' is necessary to understand the 'why' behind the negative satisfaction scores, adding crucial depth to the 'experiment_results'.

subject: CS-HCI
description: The team writes the discussion section of the paper, synthesizing the quantitative performance gains with the qualitative findings on user dissatisfaction. They propose that the decrease in satisfaction is linked to users feeling a loss of control and predictability, a key insight derived from the follow-up analysis.
reason: After the follow-up analysis in e_10 provided an explanation for the unexpected results, the logical next step is to write the discussion section of the paper. This 'writing' event integrates the quantitative and qualitative 'experiment_results' to interpret the study's findings in the context of the original 'research_goals', moving the paper closer to completion.

subject: CS-HCI
description: Based on the key finding that adaptive UIs can hurt user satisfaction by reducing their sense of control, the team revisits the project's high-level goals. They decide to pivot the research focus towards investigating 'mixed-initiative' adaptive UIs that balance system-driven adaptation with explicit user control, updating the long-term research plan.
reason: The surprising findings from the main experiment (e_10, e_11) represent a major turning point. It's a natural and critical step to return to the 'proposal' stage to update the 'research_goals' and 'research_plan' to reflect this new, more nuanced understanding of the problem, setting a clear direction for subsequent research cycles.

subject: CS-HCI
description: The team begins to design a new experimental scheme to investigate the 'mixed-initiative' concept. This involves brainstorming and prototyping different ways to provide users with control over the UI adaptation, such as offering suggestions, allowing overrides, or providing a control panel to tune the adaptation level.
reason: After the project's goals were updated in e_12 to focus on mixed-initiative systems, the logical next step is to design a new experiment to test this concept. This 'scheme_design' event translates the revised 'research_goals' into a concrete 'method_scheme' for the next round of experiments, building on the lessons learned from previous 'experiment_results'.

subject: CS-HCI
description: Run a small-scale pilot study for the new 'mixed-initiative' UI concept. A few users interact with prototypes that allow them to override or accept UI adaptations, providing early feedback on whether this approach successfully improves their sense of control and satisfaction.
reason: After designing a new experimental scheme in e_13 to test the revised 'mixed-initiative' research goals, the most logical and prudent next step is to conduct 'pilot_experiments'. This will validate the feasibility of the new method and prototypes before committing to a larger, more resource-intensive main study.

subject: CS-HCI
description: Analyze the results from the mixed-initiative pilot study. The analysis focuses on whether giving users override capabilities improved their reported satisfaction and sense of control, comparing this new data against the findings from the first main experiment.
reason: After conducting the new pilot experiment (e_14), the immediate and necessary next step is to perform an 'analysis' of the collected 'experiment_results'. This is critical for evaluating the success of the new mixed-initiative 'method_scheme' and deciding whether to proceed to a second main experiment or to further refine the design.

subject: CS-HCI
description: The analysis of the mixed-initiative pilot study was promising, indicating improved user satisfaction. The team now launches the second main experiment with a larger participant pool to robustly validate the effectiveness of the new mixed-initiative adaptive UI against the previous fully-automatic version and a static baseline.
reason: Following the positive analysis of the second pilot study (e_15), the logical next step is to conduct a full-scale 'main_experiments'. This will gather conclusive data to validate the new mixed-initiative 'method_scheme' and address the updated 'research_goals' before concluding the project.

subject: CS-HCI
description: Conduct a final, comprehensive analysis comparing the results from the second main experiment (mixed-initiative) with the first (fully-automatic) and the static baseline. The analysis confirms that the mixed-initiative approach successfully improves user satisfaction and sense of control without significantly sacrificing the performance gains of the fully-automatic system.
reason: After completing the second main experiment (e_16), the essential next step is to perform a detailed 'analysis' of the collected data. This will provide the definitive results needed to answer the revised 'research_goals' and determine the success of the mixed-initiative approach, forming the core conclusion of the entire research project.

subject: CS-HCI
description: The team restructures and rewrites the paper to present a cohesive narrative across both major experiments. The introduction and conclusion are drafted to frame the first experiment as identifying a key problem (satisfaction vs. performance) and the second experiment as successfully testing a solution (the mixed-initiative model).
reason: With the final, conclusive analysis from the second main experiment complete (e_17), the project has a full story. The next step is to consolidate all findings into a final paper draft. This 'writing' event integrates the results from both experimental cycles into a single narrative, moving the project into its final dissemination stage.

subject: CS-HCI
description: The team conducts a full review of the entire paper manuscript. They polish the writing, ensure a consistent narrative flow, finalize all figures and tables, and write the abstract and acknowledgements, preparing the paper for submission to a conference or journal.
reason: After restructuring the paper in e_18 to create a cohesive narrative, the next logical step is a final 'writing' phase to polish the entire manuscript. This involves reviewing the complete text and finalizing all components, ensuring it is ready for submission, which is the culmination of the research effort.

subject: CS-HCI
description: The final manuscript has been submitted to a top-tier HCI conference. The core research activities for this project are concluded, pending peer review feedback which might inform future work.
reason: The project has completed two full research cycles, from identifying a problem (e_10) to proposing and validating a solution (e_17). The entire narrative has been captured in a polished manuscript (e_19), fulfilling the project's revised goals. The research story is complete, making this a natural conclusion for the timeline.
